target.title: fix(cron): add lane progress heartbeat during embedded agent tool execution (fixes #94033)
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: Cron jobs with `isolated: true` fail with `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` when tool execution exceeds the sliding window, even though `timeoutSeconds` is configured appropriately. The root cause is that `noteLaneTaskProgress()` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` is only called during `notifyExecutionPhase` (startup) and `notifyRunProgress` (attempt progress events), but NOT during long-running tool execution (e.g., `exec` commands running 5+ minutes). The command-lane sliding window (`laneTaskTimeoutMs = timeoutMs + 30s grace`) expires because no progress heartbeat runs while tools execute.

**Solution**: Wrap the `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend()` call (currently at line 1856) in an IIFE that starts a `setInterval(noteLaneTaskProgress, 30000)` before the attempt and clears it via `clearInterval` in `.finally()`. This keeps lane progress alive during long tool execution without affecting the cron/agent absolute timeout or lane cleanup semantics.

**Root cause**: `noteLaneTaskProgress()` (defined at line 641, called only in `notifyExecutionPhase` at line 778 and `notifyRunProgress` at line 784) is not invoked during the `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend()` await (lines 1856-2018). The attempt itself may execute tools for many minutes, during which no progress heartbeat fires, allowing the sliding-window lane timeout to expire.

**What changed**: Added an IIFE wrapper around `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend()` that periodically calls `noteLaneTaskProgress()` every 30 seconds via `setInterval`, with `clearInterval` in the `.finally()` handler to clean up the interval when the attempt completes or errors.

## Reproduction

1. Configure a cron job with `isolated: true` and `timeoutSeconds: 600`
2. The cron job executes a tool (e.g., `exec` running a build script) that takes more than ~10 minutes
3. Observe: `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError: Lane task timed out after 630000ms` triggers at approximately 10.5 minutes after the last progress update, even though the tool is still running normally
4. The lane timeout fires because `noteLaneTaskProgress()` was last called during startup/attempt initialization, not during tool execution

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (#94033)**: Cron isolated agent `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` during long tool execution: `noteLaneTaskProgress()` was only called in `notifyExecutionPhase` and `notifyRunProgress`, not during tool execution. The sliding-window lane timeout (`laneTaskTimeoutMs = timeoutMs + 30s`) expired because no progress heartbeat ran while tools executed. Fix wraps `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend()` call in an IIFE with `setInterval(noteLaneTaskProgress, 30000)`, adding a `clearInterval` in `.finally()` to keep lane progress alive during long-running tool execution.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 — Fake timers against `enqueueCommandInLane` sliding window and `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.lane-timeout-heartbeat.test.ts src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.compaction-loop-guard.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.process.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/0668001395/openclawProject1


 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
   Start at  17:51:52
   Duration  540ms (transform 160ms, setup 38ms, import 231ms, tests 68ms, environment 0ms)

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/0668001395/openclawProject1


 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
   Start at  17:51:57
   Duration  10.31s (transform 5.47s, setup 667ms, import 367ms, tests 9.62s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 23.93s
```

**Observed result after fix**: The IIFE `setInterval(noteLaneTaskProgress, 30000)` heartbeat renews the lane progress timestamp every 30s during `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend`. The command-lane sliding window reads the updated progress timestamp and never accumulates enough elapsed time to trigger `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError`. Long tool execution completes without spurious lane timeout; the cron/agent absolute timeout still governs overall run duration.

**What was not tested**: Live 10-minute cron isolated-agent run with a long exec tool (prohibitively slow for CI); cron outer timeout vs lane heartbeat interaction at the exact 30s boundary; production cluster scheduling with overlapping isolated cron runs; behavior when the heartbeat `setInterval` callback itself throws or hangs.

**Repro confirmation**: Before fix: the `run.lane-timeout-heartbeat.test.ts` file exercises the command-lane sliding window without periodic progress renewal — the lane times out (matching the `CommandLaneTaskTimeoutError` report). ClawSweeper source-repro at commit f3ae5252115e confirmed the missing progress heartbeat on main. After fix: same commands run, the sliding window is kept alive by periodic progress heartbeat, and the lane no longer times out during long execution. Existing command-queue and compaction-loop-guard suites complete without regression.

## Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: The `setInterval` heartbeat could mask a genuinely stuck embedded attempt by indefinitely renewing lane progress.
  **Mitigation**: The cron/agent absolute timeout (`timeoutMs` passed to `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend`) still governs overall run duration. The lane timeout is a sliding-window mechanism; the heartbeat only prevents spurious lane-level timeouts during legitimate tool execution. The agent-level timeout fires after `timeoutMs` regardless of lane progress.

- **Risk**: The `setInterval` callback could throw or the interval could not be cleared on certain error paths.
  **Mitigation**: `noteLaneTaskProgress()` is a simple assignment (`laneTaskProgressAtMs = Date.now()`) that cannot throw. `clearInterval` is placed in `.finally()` which runs unconditionally (success, error, or abort). The `parentAbortSignal.removeEventListener` call remains in the same `.finally()` block, preserving the existing abort relay cleanup.

- **Risk**: Behavior change at the exact 30s boundary between lane heartbeat and cron absolute timeout.
  **Mitigation**: The cron timeout (absolute) and lane timeout (sliding, heartbeat-renewed) are independent mechanisms. The cron timeout aborts the underlying agent run; the lane heartbeat only prevents premature cancellation during active tool execution. The 30s heartbeat interval is well within the 30s lane grace period, providing a comfortable margin.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (pure logic, no new types/interfaces/fields)
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Documentation

## Scope

- **Files changed**: 1 (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts`)
- **Lines**: ~15 (IIFE wrapper + setInterval + clearInterval in .finally())
- **Subsystem**: `cron/embedded-agent-runner` — lane task progress heartbeat
- **Backward compatible**: Yes — no API changes, no type changes, no behavior change for fast tool execution

## Regression Test Plan

1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.lane-timeout-heartbeat.test.ts` — new tests covering lane timeout sliding window, progress renewal, and periodic heartbeat prevention
2. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/process/command-queue.test.ts` — existing 30 tests covering command-lane timeout, progress renewal, lane draining, and edge cases
3. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.compaction-loop-guard.test.ts` — existing 6 tests covering post-compaction loop guard and embedded run orchestration
4. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts` — cron isolated-agent error status handling
5. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` — cron timer regression suite

## Review Findings Addressed

- [ClawSweeper review](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94033#issuecomment-4727926369): Source-level reproduction confirmed. The fix addresses the specific missing `noteLaneTaskProgress()` call during `runEmbeddedAttemptWithBackend` execution.
- The fix preserves cron absolute timeout and lane cleanup semantics — the heartbeat is bounded by the attempt lifecycle (cleared in `.finally()`).
- No changes to `src/process/command-queue.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`, or `src/cron/service/agent-watchdog.ts` — adjacent subsystems are unaffected.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes: #94033
- Related PR (adjacent, pre-execution watchdog): #93914 (does not address the long in-flight tool execution path)

target.title: fix: making typing start fire-and-forget allows cleanup/idle to run before a persistent typin...
target.body:
## Summary

Found one concrete regression: making typing start fire-and-forget allows cleanup/idle to run before a persistent typing indicator has finished starting, so the stop path can no-op and leave the indicator behind.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Medium: Pending typing start can outlive cleanup and leave persistent indicators stuck** (regression)
  - File: `src/channels/typing.ts:79`
  - Evidence: `onReplyStart` now starts `fireStart()` in the background and returns after one microtask at `src/channels/typing.ts:79-87`, while `fireStop` immediately marks the callbacks closed and calls `stop` at `src/channels/typing.ts:90-98`. For Feishu, `start` assigns `typingState` only after `await addTypingIndicator(...)` at `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts:176`; `stop` returns without doing anything if `typingState` is still unset at `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts:183-185`. A fast cleanup while `addTypingIndicator` is still in flight therefore calls stop too early, then the delayed start completes and records a persistent reaction with no later removal.
  - Impact: Feishu typing reactions are explicitly persistent until removed. A quick reply, `NO_REPLY`, empty-message cleanup, cancellation, or send-policy path can leave a visible typing reaction/indicator on the parent message after the run is already finished.
  - Suggested fix: keep typing start off the reply critical path, but serialize cleanup against the pending start. For example, track the current start promise and, if stop/cleanup happens while it is pending, run `stop` again after the promise settles when a start actually completed. Alternatively make persistent-indicator channels expose an abortable/pending-aware start helper.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `src/channels/typing.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/typing.ts`
- `src/channels/typing.test.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d
- Latest main at intake: 8093ae60292f4d9009b3d48f234b39b5cbc538e5
- Original commit author: Ayaan Zaidi
- GitHub author: @obviyus
- Highest severity: medium
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `40b0b1bfe051072f47d0160eb078afa3b17cef48..45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d`
- Changed files: `src/auto-reply/reply/typing.ts`, `src/channels/typing.ts`, `src/channels/typing.test.ts`
- Code read: changed files in full; `src/channels/typing-lifecycle.ts`; `src/channels/typing-start-guard.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`; relevant channel typing call sites, including Feishu, Matrix, Telegram, Discord, Signal, Mattermost, MSTeams, Zalo, and heartbeat typing.
- Dependencies/web: no dependency files changed; no web lookup needed.

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm install` was needed because `vitest/package.json` was missing.
- `pnpm test src/channels/typing.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/typing-persistence.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` passed.
- Focused `pnpm exec tsx -e ...` race probe reproduced the stale persistent-state ordering.

Known review limits:
- I did not run a live Feishu API test; the finding is based on the current callback ordering and Feishu’s local persistent-reaction state contract.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.

- Source PRs: none
- Credit: Detected by ClawSweeper commit review for 45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d.; Original commit author: Ayaan Zaidi.

target.title: qa-lab: add evidence artifact gallery
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds a QA Lab evidence gallery for existing `qa-evidence.json` outputs.

- Adds a server-side evidence model that loads QA evidence entries, declared artifacts, status counts, coverage IDs, text/json previews, and optional producer context.
- Adds `/api/evidence` and `/api/evidence/artifact` routes so the QA Lab web app can inspect artifacts without exposing arbitrary repo files.
- Adds an Evidence Archive tab in the QA Lab web app with filters, artifact previews, downloadable declared artifacts, and drill-down details.
- Hardens artifact resolution and artifact responses after review: missing bundle-local artifacts no longer fall back to repo-root files, malformed optional matrix cells are ignored, artifact responses are `no-store`/`nosniff`, and undeclared/outside artifacts return controlled client errors.
- Intentionally does not add the UX matrix runner, UX matrix scenario, or GitHub Action wiring. Those are follow-up PR slices.

Success looks like maintainers can open a QA Lab evidence artifact from a suite run and inspect screenshots, videos, logs, json validation output, coverage IDs, and run context from the web app.

Reviewers should focus on path containment/artifact authorization, the evidence model shape, and whether the UI is generic enough for QA Lab evidence beyond the UX matrix producer.

## Linked context

Related #94276
Related #94273

This split follows maintainer feedback to pull the original combined QA Lab / UX matrix work apart:

- PR 1: script-backed QA scenarios
- PR 2: QA web app evidence gallery
- PR 3: UX matrix test/scenario
- possible PR 4: GitHub Action/smoke profile wiring

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: QA Lab can load and inspect a QA evidence artifact bundle through the web server instead of requiring people to read raw `qa-evidence.json` directly.
- Real environment tested: local macOS OpenClaw checkout, branch `codex/qa-lab-evidence-gallery-main`, head `c0cfa19422`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx -e "import fs from 'node:fs/promises'; import path from 'node:path'; import { chromium } from 'playwright'; import { startQaLabServer } from './extensions/qa-lab/src/lab-server.ts'; const repoRoot = process.cwd(); const evidencePath = '.artifacts/ux-matrix/20260617T203150Z-8ac4664d16/qa-evidence.json'; const proofDir = path.join(repoRoot, '.artifacts', 'qa-lab-evidence-gallery-proof', '20260617T2258Z-142f7b234d'); await fs.mkdir(proofDir, { recursive: true }); const server = await startQaLabServer({ repoRoot, host: '127.0.0.1', port: 0, embeddedGateway: 'disabled' }); let browser; try { browser = await chromium.launch({ headless: true }); const context = await browser.newContext({ viewport: { width: 1440, height: 1050 }, recordVideo: { dir: proofDir, size: { width: 1440, height: 1050 } } }); const page = await context.newPage(); const url = server.baseUrl + '/evidence?path=' + encodeURIComponent(evidencePath); await page.goto(url, { waitUntil: 'networkidle' }); await page.getByText('Evidence Archive').first().waitFor({ state: 'visible', timeout: 10000 }); await page.locator('.evidence-entry-card').first().waitFor({ state: 'visible', timeout: 10000 }); await page.locator('.evidence-artifact-card').first().waitFor({ state: 'visible', timeout: 10000 }); const screenshotPath = path.join(proofDir, 'evidence-archive-loaded.png'); await page.screenshot({ path: screenshotPath, fullPage: true }); const entryCount = await page.locator('.evidence-entry-card').count(); const artifactCount = await page.locator('.evidence-artifact-card').count(); const matrixCellCount = await page.locator('.evidence-matrix-cell').count(); const title = await page.title(); await context.close(); const videos = (await fs.readdir(proofDir)).filter((file) => file.endsWith('.webm')); console.log(JSON.stringify({ baseUrl: server.baseUrl, url, title, evidencePath, screenshotPath, videos: videos.map((file) => path.join(proofDir, file)), entryCount, artifactCount, matrixCellCount }, null, 2)); } finally { if (browser) await browser.close().catch(() => {}); await server.stop(); }"
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
{
  "baseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:62325",
  "url": "http://127.0.0.1:62325/evidence?path=.artifacts%2Fux-matrix%2F20260617T203150Z-8ac4664d16%2Fqa-evidence.json",
  "title": "QA Lab",
  "evidencePath": ".artifacts/ux-matrix/20260617T203150Z-8ac4664d16/qa-evidence.json",
  "screenshotPath": ".artifacts/qa-lab-evidence-gallery-proof/20260617T2258Z-142f7b234d/evidence-archive-loaded.png",
  "videos": [
    ".artifacts/qa-lab-evidence-gallery-proof/20260617T2258Z-142f7b234d/page@e5b70b25e6941bd445638c67995b7ee4.webm"
  ],
  "entryCount": 4,
  "artifactCount": 5,
  "matrixCellCount": 135
}
```

- Observed result after fix: QA Lab opened the Evidence Archive route in a real browser, loaded a saved UX Matrix `qa-evidence.json`, rendered evidence entries, artifact cards, producer context, a scorecard preview, artifact links, and the UX matrix mini-grid.
- What was not tested: the future UX matrix scenario PR and GitHub Actions wiring.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this proof used an existing local QA evidence artifact generated by earlier UX matrix work; it did not generate a fresh suite run as part of this PR, and the screenshot/video artifacts are local proof outputs rather than committed repo files.
- Before evidence: before this PR, QA Lab did not expose a generic Evidence Archive tab/API for loading and browsing QA evidence artifacts from the web app.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```bash
pnpm run lint:tmp:no-raw-channel-fetch
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-gallery.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/lab-server.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/web/src/ui-render.test.ts
pnpm qa:lab:build
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental false
pnpm lint --threads=8
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
```

Regression coverage added:

- Evidence model tests for loading summaries, declared artifacts, producer files, missing artifacts, path containment, missing bundle-local artifacts, undeclared artifacts, symlink escapes, and malformed optional matrix cells.
- Lab server tests for artifact `HEAD`/`GET`, `no-store`/`nosniff` response headers, undeclared artifacts, and outside artifact requests.
- Web UI render tests for evidence filters, artifact cards, deferred media previews, blocked/skipped statuses, and drill-down state.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`

Highest-risk area: serving local artifact files through the QA Lab server.

Mitigation: artifact downloads are constrained to declared artifacts from the selected `qa-evidence.json`, resolved inside the repo/evidence roots, served with `no-store` and `nosniff`, and covered by path containment/negative HTTP tests.

## Current review state

Next action: CI rerun and ClawSweeper/reviewer re-review.

Waiting on: GitHub CI and reviewer feedback after `c0cfa19422`.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: raw fetch boundary line drift, artifact response hardening, missing bundle-local artifact fallback, malformed matrix cell parsing, controlled API status mapping, canonical evidence path URLs, `noopener noreferrer` artifact links, producer-file manifest duplication, shared `toRepoRelativePath`, bounded artifact view concurrency, shared Evidence DTO type aliases, and cheap Evidence Archive IA label/subtitle.

target.title: feat(feishu): comprehensive plugin enhancements — streaming, dedup, skills, calendar, and stability
target.body:
## Summary

This PR consolidates all Feishu plugin enhancements developed across multiple iterations (supersedes #38770, #38772, #39409, #39440). All previous PRs were closed without merging; this PR contains the complete changeset.

---

## Issues Fixed

### Streaming Card Display

1. **Text Duplication in Streaming Cards**: `mergeStreamingText()` fallback path duplicated cumulative text → bypass it, treat `onPartialReply` text as complete state
2. **Content Duplication Across Cards**: each card showed cumulative text instead of its own block → track `blockOffset`, slice cumulative text per card
3. **Content Disappearing on Card Close**: `closeStreaming()` used per-block text while card displayed cumulative text → `streamText` consistently uses sliced card text
4. **Missing Tail Text After Last Block**: `deliver(kind=final)` sent full duplicate or was skipped → extract only undelivered tail `text.slice(blockOffset)`, send if non-empty
5. **Extra Streaming Card After Last Block**: `onPartialReply` kept creating new cards → only start new card if substantial new content beyond `blockOffset`
6. **Slow Streaming Updates**: `await this.queue` blocked `onPartialReply` on API round-trips; no flush timer → remove `await` (fire-and-forget), add `pendingFlushTimer` (300ms auto-flush)
7. **Markdown Crash Guard**: `isSafeToRender()` blocks updates during unclosed code fences
8. **Typing Indicator Emoji**: Changed from `Typing` to `Get` (fix reaction 231001 error)
9. **Broken CardKit Streaming**: Replaced broken CardKit chunked patching with Semantic Block Delivery using `block` events from `reply-dispatcher.ts`

### Message Deduplication (3-Layer)

10. **event_id Dedup**: Lark SDK `EventDispatcher.parse()` flattens header into callback data — `event_id` IS available. Replaced content-hash workaround with proper `event_id` dedup as recommended by Feishu docs
11. **Stale Message Filtering in Debounce**: When debounce batch has messages with >2min time gap, drop older messages and record their IDs in persistent dedup
12. **create_time-based Stale Rejection**: Feishu retries re-deliver messages with new `message_id`/`event_id` but same `create_time`. Track last processed `create_time` per `chat+sender` and reject stale duplicates

### Message Control & Abort

13. **Fast Abort Bypass**: Trigger words (`stop`, `停止`, `取消`, `esc`, etc.) skip 2s debounce + bypass `chatQueue` → direct dispatch to `tryFastAbortFromMessage`
14. **Message Recall Abort**: Register `im.message.recalled_v1` → clear debounce buffer, record in dedup, synthesize `/stop` command via `dispatchFeishuMessageDirect`

### Feishu Ops Tools (Skills)

15. **feishu_contacts**: Search contacts with auto-sync from API
16. **feishu_groups**: List/search bot groups with auto-sync
17. **feishu_send**: Send text/file/@all to users or groups
18. **feishu_calendar**: Create, **update**, and **delete** calendar events by `event_id`
19. **2-Tier Contact Sync**: When list API returns 0 results, fallback to BFS department traversal + per-department user fetch (ported from `feishu-ops/scripts/update-contacts.py`)
20. **SKILL.md Rewrite**: All skill docs converted to English with JSON tool-call examples

### Code Quality

21. **Error Messages Standardized to English** across all new modules
22. **Group Search Hint**: `searchGroupsOrSync()` lists all bot groups and prompts user to add bot to target group if not found
23. **Mention Logic**: More resilient verify mention logic and fix log propagation
24. **SDK v1.30+ Response Parsing**: Fix `proactive-send.ts` to handle Lark SDK top-level response format (`{ file_key }` instead of `{ code: 0, data: { file_key } }`)

### Other

25. **Daemon Working Directory**: Resolve gateway working directory to installation root

---

## Files Changed

| File | Changes |
|---|---|
| `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` | event_id dedup, create_time dedup, fast abort bypass, recall handler, mention logic |
| `extensions/feishu/src/dedup.ts` | `checkAndRecordCreateTime()`, create_time tracking per chat+sender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts` | Block-boundary card splitting, blockOffset tracking, tail text, semantic block delivery |
| `extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts` | Remove mergeStreamingText, async queue, pendingFlushTimer, isSafeToRender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/typing.ts` | Change typing emoji to Get |
| `extensions/feishu/src/inbound-debounce.ts` | Stale message filtering, removeFromBuffer() |
| `extensions/feishu/src/feishu-ops-tools.ts` | Register contacts, groups, send, calendar tools |
| `extensions/feishu/src/contacts.ts` | 2-tier sync with department fallback |
| `extensions/feishu/src/calendar.ts` | Update and delete calendar events |
| `extensions/feishu/src/proactive-send.ts` | SDK v1.30+ response parsing fix |
| `extensions/feishu/skills/*/SKILL.md` | English rewrite with JSON examples |
| `src/daemon/program-args.ts` | Working directory resolution fix |

## Supersedes

- #38770 (CLOSED — streaming card fix)
- #38772 (CLOSED — streaming + event_id dedup)
- #39409 (CLOSED — message control, skills, stability)
- #39440 (CLOSED — calendar update/delete)

target.title: fix(gateway): forward child session node events to spawnedBy subscribers
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway node subscriptions are keyed by exact `sessionKey`, so child-session node events disappear when only the parent session is subscribed.
- Why it matters: spawned agent chat activity can look dead from the gateway/node subscriber side even while work is actually running.
- What changed: added a direct-miss fallback in `createNodeSubscriptionManager.sendToSession()` that walks `spawnedBy` lineage, canonicalizes ancestor keys, and routes to the first ancestor with subscribers; added a focused regression test and changelog entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change ACP execution, session transcript bridging, or broader subagent progress/status surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #34852
- Related #34905

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Spawned child-session node events now reach parent-session node subscribers when only the parent session is subscribed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway node subscriptions
- Relevant config (redacted): default test harness

### Steps

1. Subscribe a node to a parent session key.
2. Emit a node event under a spawned child session key whose `spawnedBy` points to that parent.
3. Observe delivery through the node subscription manager.

### Expected

- Parent subscriber receives the child-session event.

### Actual

- Before this fix, delivery stopped on the child key miss and no event was sent.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: child-session events fall back through `spawnedBy` lineage to a subscribed parent; canonicalized parent lookup works; repeated sends reuse the cached ancestor lookup.
- Edge cases checked: no direct subscriber regression; partial object assertion accounts for `payloadJSON`; focused gateway test suite passes locally.
- What you did **not** verify: full gateway end-to-end node streaming against a live spawned-agent session.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR to restore exact-key-only subscription routing.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-node-subscriptions.ts`, `src/gateway/gateway-misc.test.ts`, `CHANGELOG.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: node subscribers receiving unexpected ancestor-routed events beyond the intended `spawnedBy` chain.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: lineage fallback could repeatedly touch session storage on hot miss paths.
  - Mitigation: fallback only runs on direct misses, uses a bounded depth, and caches the resolved ancestor subscriber key.

Supersedes the dirty branch state in #34905.

target.title: fix(agents): normalize mangled tool names and IDs from OpenAI-compati…
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Some OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g. Kimi) send tool call IDs like `functions.exec:0` as `functions exec:0` (space instead of dot) and tool names like `exec` as `functions exec`. Tool result matching fails, causing "Tool functions exec not found" errors.
- **Why it matters:** Users on Kimi and similar providers cannot complete tool-using flows; matching is done by ID and name, and the mangled forms did not match.
- **What changed:** Added `normalizeMangledToolCallId()` in `tool-call-id.ts` to rewrite `functions ` -> `functions.` in IDs; use it in `extractToolResultId()` and as the key in `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist()` so mangled and canonical IDs map to the same sanitized value. Added `normalizePrefixedToolName()` in `session-transcript-repair.ts` to strip `functions.` / `functions ` from tool names; applied in assistant block handling, `hasToolCallName`, and `normalizeToolResultName()` so tool-result names are normalized and pairing works.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No change to provider clients or wire format; only transcript repair and ID sanitization for matching.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39091
- Related: normalizeToolResultName fix included

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Tool-using flows with OpenAI-compatible providers that mangle tool IDs/names (e.g. Kimi) now match tool results to tool calls correctly; "Tool functions exec not found" is resolved.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Provider: OpenAI-compatible (e.g. Kimi) that sends `functions exec:0` / `functions exec` style IDs and names

### Steps

1. Run agent with such a provider; trigger a tool call (e.g. exec).
2. Before fix: tool result may not match, "Tool functions exec not found" or similar.
3. After fix: IDs and names normalize; pairing and sanitization map mangled and canonical forms to the same value.

### Expected

Tool results pair with assistant tool calls; no spurious "tool not found" from ID/name mangling.

### Actual

As expected after the fix.

## Evidence

- `normalizeMangledToolCallId` and `extractToolResultId` / `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist` tests in `tool-call-id.test.ts`.
- `sanitizeToolUseResultPairing` test with mangled ID and prefixed name in `session-transcript-repair.test.ts`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Unit tests added; logic follows issue and Greptile feedback (compare original rawToolName in normalizeToolResultName, use normalized ID as map key).
- **Edge cases checked:** Empty string, already-canonical IDs, names without prefix.
- **What you did not verify:** Live Kimi or other provider E2E.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert the commit.
- Files: `src/agents/tool-call-id.ts`, `src/agents/session-transcript-repair.ts`.

## Risks and Mitigations

**None.** Normalization is additive and only affects matching/sanitization; no new execution paths.

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent outbound A2A allowlist override
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `agents.list[].tools.agentToAgent.allow` to control which agents a specific agent may contact via session tools (`sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, `session_status`)
- When unset, the agent falls back to the global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` list; when set to explicit IDs, only those targets are reachable; `[]` isolates the agent from all cross-agent access
- The global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` continues to act as a participation gate (both requester and target must match it) before per-agent outbound rules are evaluated
- Outbound-only semantics: only the requester's override is checked, not the target's
- Replace `matchesAllow` with `matchesGlobalParticipation` and add `evaluateAccess()` returning a structured `AgentToAgentDenyReason` so all call sites produce context-aware error messages via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`
- Scrub deleted agent references from per-agent outbound allowlists in `pruneAgentConfig()`

### Example config (hub-spoke pattern)

```json
{
  "tools": {
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      { "id": "david",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["*"] } } },
      { "id": "spoke",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } },
      { "id": "kelly",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } }
    ]
  }
}
```

spoke→david ✅ | spoke→kelly ❌ | david→anyone ✅

## Test plan

- [x] `sessions-access.test.ts`: per-agent override, deny-all `[]`, global gate, target override ignored (outbound-only), fallback to global when unset
- [x] `sessions.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny during label lookup in `sessions_send`
- [x] `openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny for cross-agent `session_status`
- [x] `agents.test.ts`: `pruneAgentConfig` scrubs deleted agent from per-agent allowlists
- [x] Build passes
- [x] Manual: configure hub-spoke agents (david/spoke/kelly), verify per-agent outbound rules — see `Real behavior proof` section below

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: per-agent outbound `agentToAgent.allow` correctly gates cross-agent session tool access. Hub-spoke topology used: `david` (hub, `allow=["*"]`), `spoke` (allow=`["david"]`), `kelly` (allow=`["david"]`). Validates the new `evaluateAccess()` decision pipeline reaches the documented per-agent outbound semantics, with the structured `per_agent_outbound` deny reason surfaced via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`.

- **Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 22.04 (kernel 5.15), Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10. Branch HEAD `55819f6f43` of `repair/pr-39102-a2a-refresh` (this PR rebased on current main). Live invocation of the production export `createAgentToAgentPolicy` from `src/plugin-sdk/session-visibility.ts` — no mocks, no test runner, the same code path that backs `sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, and `session_status` at runtime.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  1. Author a hub-spoke `OpenClawConfig` matching the docs example (david hub + spoke + kelly).
  2. Build `policy = createAgentToAgentPolicy(cfg)`.
  3. Call `policy.evaluateAccess(requester, target)` for the five edge pairs.
  4. Run with: `pnpm tsx proof-hub-spoke.ts` (the script imports the worktree source directly so the running code is the patched export).

- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output, copied verbatim from `node` stdout via `tsx`.

  ```
  # per-agent A2A allowlist — hub-spoke proof
  # config: david=hub (allow *), spoke=spoke (allow david), kelly=spoke (allow david)

  [ok ] spoke -> david: ALLOWED
  [ok ] spoke -> kelly: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)
  [ok ] david -> kelly: ALLOWED
  [ok ] david -> spoke: ALLOWED
  [ok ] kelly -> spoke: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)

  all cases match expected behavior
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: all five real invocations matched the documented hub-spoke semantics. Cross-spoke calls (`spoke -> kelly`, `kelly -> spoke`) were denied with the structured `per_agent_outbound` reason code from `AgentToAgentDecision`. Hub-rooted calls (`david -> spoke`, `david -> kelly`) and spoke-to-hub (`spoke -> david`) were allowed. Process exit code: `0`. Console output above is the literal stdout, no post-processing.

- **What was not tested**: end-to-end IPC roundtrip through the live `sessions_send` agent process pair (single-machine ACP transport). The four session call sites all delegate the access decision to the single `evaluateAccess()` policy proven above, and the wiring of each call site is covered by the unit tests already listed in the Test plan.

target.title: Security: add configurable unpaired DM responses
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: unknown DM senders in pairing mode always received a branded OpenClaw reply with a pairing code.
- Why it matters: operators could not choose a silent or de-branded pairing posture without changing DM policy semantics.
- What changed: added `unpairedResponse` config support (`silent | code-only | branded`) and wired it through the shared pairing helpers and core DM pairing flows.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default behavior remains `branded`, and non-pairing DM policies are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #29945
- Related #29945

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional `unpairedResponse` config for DM pairing flows on core messaging channels.
- `silent` suppresses the automatic reply, `code-only` removes OpenClaw branding, and `branded` preserves current behavior.
- Defaults stay backward compatible: existing installs continue to send the branded pairing reply unless configured otherwise.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp, Discord, Telegram, Slack, Signal, iMessage, LINE, plugin runtime pairing callers
- Relevant config (redacted): default config plus `unpairedResponse` overrides in channel config

### Steps

1. Configure a DM channel with `dmPolicy: pairing`.
2. Send a DM from an unapproved sender.
3. Set `unpairedResponse` to `silent`, `code-only`, and `branded` and compare the emitted reply behavior.

### Expected

- Pairing mode should respect the configured reply style without changing allowlist/pairing authorization behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, pairing replies were always branded and could not be suppressed or de-branded.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: new `buildPairingReply` modes and silent-path behavior in `issuePairingChallenge`; end-to-end build completed after wiring core channel call sites.
- Edge cases checked: silent mode skips outbound reply send; code-only mode still includes the pairing code and approval command; branded mode remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live channel delivery against external services.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `unpairedResponse` from config to fall back to the existing branded reply, or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: channel config entries using `unpairedResponse`; pairing helper changes in `src/pairing/*`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: pairing requests created without a reply when `unpairedResponse` is not set, or null reply handling regressions in DM pairing paths.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a DM pairing caller could miss the new nullable reply contract and fail to send or crash.
  - Mitigation: updated the shared helper plus direct call sites, covered the shared helper with tests, and validated with `pnpm build` and `pnpm check`.

target.title: Security: harden gateway timeouts and auth store sealing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `GatewayClient.request()` could hang indefinitely, auth stores were always plaintext at rest, and mirrored transcript writes did not re-assert restrictive permissions after append.
- Why it matters: hung gateway RPCs leak pending state and stall callers; optional at-rest sealing gives operators a low-friction hardening path for OpenClaw-owned auth JSON stores.
- What changed: added bounded gateway request timeouts, added optional `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` sealing for `auth-profiles.json` and legacy `oauth.json`, added regression coverage, and re-applied `0600` perms after mirrored transcript writes.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not implement full session transcript encryption because `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` and multiple local readers still depend on raw JSONL session files.
- Thanks @alamine42 for the original hardening report, and @vincentkoc for review context.

## Change Type (select all)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #4954
- Related #7604

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `GatewayClient` requests now fail with `gateway request timeout for <method>` instead of hanging forever.
- When `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` is set, OpenClaw-owned auth stores are written in sealed form and require that same env var to be read.
- Mirrored transcript writes now best-effort reset the transcript file mode to `0600` after append.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  Optional auth-store sealing adds a new env-based operator hardening path. The format is backward compatible because plaintext reads still work when sealing is not enabled, and encrypted reads fail closed with a warning if the passphrase env var is missing.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: n/a
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a
- Relevant config (redacted): optional `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE`

### Steps

1. Run the focused test suite for gateway client, auth store, oauth migration, and transcript paths.
2. Run repo-level `pnpm check`.
3. Run repo-level `pnpm build`.

### Expected

- Gateway requests time out cleanly.
- Auth stores round-trip in sealed form when `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` is set.
- Build and lint stay green.

### Actual

- Verified locally.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: focused Vitest coverage for gateway timeouts, sealed auth-store save/load, sealed oauth migration, and transcript append behavior.
- Edge cases checked: timeout cleanup removes pending entries; encrypted auth stores do not contain plaintext secrets; legacy oauth migration still works when the source file is sealed.
- What you did **not** verify: full transcript encryption, because it is explicitly out of scope for this PR.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  Operators who want auth-store sealing can set `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` before writing credentials and keep it set for future reads.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: unset `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` before writing new auth store files, or revert the branch.
- Files/config to restore: `auth-profiles.json` / `oauth.json` from backup if an operator loses the passphrase.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: warning that an encrypted auth store requires `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE` when the env var is missing.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: operator enables auth-store sealing and later starts OpenClaw without `OPENCLAW_PASSPHRASE`.
  - Mitigation: reads fail closed with a targeted warning instead of silently parsing garbage.
- Risk: timeout defaults could surface latent long-running RPC assumptions.
  - Mitigation: timeout is bounded but configurable per client/request.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix(config): dedupe warning spam and slow restart storms
target.body:
## Summary
- dedupe repeated config warning/error payloads until the config content changes again
- dedupe invalid config reload warnings so unchanged broken snapshots stop spamming logs
- slow unhealthy supervised restart storms and document the difference between rolling file logs and macOS service stdout/stderr logs

## Testing
- `pnpm vitest run src/config/io.logging.test.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts`
- `pnpm check` *(fails in pre-existing `extensions/feishu/src/media.ts` with unrelated `timeout` type errors)*

Fixes #25574
Fixes #29745

target.title: Gateway: allow extension origins in browser allowlist
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` could not explicitly allowlist `chrome-extension://` because the origin checker normalized extension origins through `URL.origin`, which becomes `"null"` for that scheme. - Why it matters: extension sidepanel clients had to rely on `["*"]` to get through browser-origin WS checks, which weakened the intended hardening. - What changed: the origin matcher now keeps exact URL parsing/validation but falls back to exact `scheme://host` matching for non-standard origins, so `chrome-extension://` can be allowlisted exactly; focused unit and handshake tests were added. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no wildcard/prefix expansion, no acceptance of literal `Origin: null`, no changes to token/device auth, host-header fallback, or non-browser client auth behavior. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #35035 (issue mentions app://* origin, but it's the same underlying problem) - Related #35396, #35057 (both PRS were fixes for this problem but were closed because the authors were banned AFAIK) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` can now contain exact extension origins such as `chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop`, and those browser-origin Gateway WS connects will succeed when otherwise authenticated. - Operators no longer need `["*"]` solely to let a Chrome extension sidepanel client connect. - Existing exact matches for normal `http(s)` origins, wildcard behavior, host-header fallback, and literal `Origin: null` rejection remain unchanged. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` (strictly speaking this allows chrome extensions/electron+tauri apps to connect but I think calling it a new capability is a stretch) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `N/A` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local Bun + Vitest - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WS browser-origin client; Chrome extension origin emulated in handshake tests via `ws` - Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.auth.mode="token"`, `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]` ### Steps 1. Configure the Gateway with token auth and `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop"]`. 2. Open a WS connection that sends `Origin: chrome-extension://abcdefghijklmnop` and a valid `connect` request. 3. Observe the handshake result. ### Expected - An exactly allowlisted extension origin should be accepted, just like an exactly allowlisted `https://...` origin. ### Actual - Before this fix, the request was rejected with `origin not allowed` because the checker collapsed the extension origin to `"null"` internally. - After this fix, the handshake succeeds and returns `hello-ok`. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: whitelisted the chrome extension I'm developing and successfully connected to the Gateway via WS - Edge cases checked: N/A - What you did **not** verify: did not try with other protocols (`app://`, `tauri://`) ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: `N/A` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit; if extension access is urgently blocked, temporarily fall back to `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins=["*"]` until the regression is understood. - Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/origin-check.ts`; optionally restore prior `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` values in config. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: an exactly allowlisted `chrome-extension://` still being rejected; malformed/non-matching non-standard origins unexpectedly being accepted; regressions in existing `http(s)` origin allowlisting.
